title: Workflow
text:
The core of a robust, reproducible analysis is a script-based workflow. This ensures that every step of your data processing is documented and can be re-executed with a single command. A well-organized project structure is the prerequisite for this automation, making your research transparent, traceable, and easy to share. Here you can see an exemplary folder structure for a script-based workflow:
Parent_Folder/
├── project.Rproj         # Project file
├── data/                 # All analysis inputs
│   └── raw/              # Raw data
├── scripts/              # All analysis scripts
│   └── analyses.qmd      # Main script
├── outputs/              # All analysis outputs
│   ├── figs/             # Graphics and plots
│   └── tables/           # Tables
├── README.md             # Brief project explanation
├── licence.txt           # License information
└── references.bib        # BibTex file


An RStudio Project file (file extension .Rproj) serves as the “anchor” for your work. It defines the root directory of your project. Such files are highly recommended to seamlessly integrate version control via Git and GitHub directly in the IDE of RStudio.



Raw data is the “source of truth” and must remain immutable. Once stored in data/raw/, these files should never be edited manually. This ensures the raw data as basement for all other parts so that these can potentially always be recovered.



As the name of the script-based workflow suggests, the script is the heart of it. It connects your raw data to your final results. Every step from data cleaning to visualization must be coded. This eliminates the risk of “forgotten” manual steps in e.g. Excel and ensures that anyone (including your future self) can reproduce your exact results.

If you have installed python on your device, it is generally possible to run both Python and R code within the same script. For the IDE of RStudio, this can be achieved by using Quarto documents (file extension .qmd). Check out the official Quarto documentation for more information on how to set up and use Python within Quarto.




All figures and tables generated by the scripts belong in the outputs/ folder. Since they are created completely automatically, they are technically “disposable”. You can delete them at any time and recreate them perfectly by re-running your scripts via a single command.



The README (file extension .md) is the manual for your project. It is the first file a collaborator or supervisor will read to understand and potentially contribute to the project. A good README should include:

Brief Description: What is the study about?
Project Structure: Where is the data and what is the order in which the scripts should be executed?
Instructions for Reproduction: Which software is required and how can the scripts be executed to reproduce the results?
Licensing: Should others cite your work, and is it allowed to reuse the code?

Note that the licensing can be stated elsewhere and is not obligatory in a README.



Make sure your work is credited accordingly so that third parties know what they are allowed to do with and what not. As stated earlier, the license can be stated within the README or in a separate file (e.g., license.txt). If you are unsure what license to use, consult your supervisor.



Quarto makes it possible to include citations automatically by using a BibTeX (file extension .bib) file.
A standard entry within a BibTex-file looks like this:
atbook{wickham2023, author = {Wickham, Hadley and Mineault, Patrick}, title = {R for Data Science}, year = {2023}, publisher = {O’Reilly Media}, edition = {2nd edition} }
The YAML header of the Quarto document should include the following lines to link the BibTeX file and the citation style file (e.g. APA 7 Style; can be found and downloaded from Zotero Style Repository):
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
You can cite the work by using the key defined in your .bib file, which is usually the surname of the first author followed by the year of publication. For example, if you want to cite the above book, you would use @wickham2023 in your text. But you can also cite the work parathetically by using [@wickham2023] in your text. The citation will then be automatically formatted according to the specified style (e.g., APA, MLA, Chicago) and included in the bibliography section of your document.
To include a bibliography section in your document, simply add the citation as described above and the Reference section will be automatically generated at the end of your document.
Wickham & Mineault (2023)

title: Syntax
text:
A codestyle is a set of conventions and rules for building script and writing the code within it. Adhering to the following rules is partly mandatory for it to run successfully or simply makes your scripts easier to read for others (and for your “future self”) and ensures that your analysis can be maintained over time.

Within a script, important meta-information can be provided using the YAML header. This includes not only the author, the date, or the integration of citations, but also formatting options for the rendered document. Here is examplary content of a YAML header:
title: “Analysis of Psychological Well-being” author: “Your Name” date: last-modified
bibliography: references.bib # Path to your bibliography file (see section “Citation” in the workflow chapter) csl: apa.csl # Path to your citation style file (see section “Citation” in the workflow chapter)
format: html: toc: true # Adds a Table of Contents number-sections: true # Numbers your headings code-fold: true # Allows readers to click to show/hide code execute: echo: true # Shows the code in the output by default (global setting) warning: false # Hides R/Python warnings in the final report (global setting)

Always load your libraries/packages at the very top of your script. This acts as a “list of ingredients” so others know what needs to be installed before running your code. Make sure you do not include any unnecessary packages, as this can lead to confusion and potential issues with package conflicts. Please also make sure to not include the installation of the package (e.g., install.packages("ggplot2")) in the script, as this can lead to issues when running the script on different devices. Instead install necessary packages directly in the Console. The loading of packages can take place within the script:

Codelibrary(ggplot2)



Python and R do not share the same packages. For data visualization, a commonly used package in Python is matplotlib. To load it, you can use the following code:

Codeimport matplotlib.pyplot as plt




In the IDE of RStudio, settings how chunks should behave globaly can be set within the YAML header. However, these settings can be overwritten for each chunk individually by adding the following options at the beginning of a chunk:
#| eval: true
#| echo: true
#| output: true
#| warning: true
#| error: true
#| include: true
The settings of a given chunk can also be set directly within the curly brackets at the beginning of a chunk, for example:
{r, eval = TRUE, echo = TRUE, output = TRUE, warning = TRUE, error = FALSE, include = FALSE}
Here are the explanations of the different options: - eval: true: The chunk is actually executed/calculated. - echo: true: The code of the chunk is visible in the rendered document. - output: true: The results of the chunk (e.g., a table) are shown. - warning: true: R warnings appear in the document. error: false: If the code within the chunk has an error, rendering stops immediately (safe mode). - include: false: The chunk, including code, output, and potential warnings or errors, is completely ignored.
Please note that this is not an extensive list of all available chunk options. For more, see for example the quarto documentation about code cells and knitr chunk options.

Avoid hardcoded values within your code. Instead, define them as variables within a single chunk at the beginning of your script. This makes it easier to update values in one place and ensures consistency throughout your code. To make it easier to identify hardcoded values in the script, you can use a specific naming convention (e.g., all uppercase letters with underscores) for these variables.

Headings are the basis for the table of content (toc) in the rendered html-file when such is enabled in the YAML header. They also help to structure the code and make it easier to navigate for others (and for your “future self”). It is recommended to use at least three levels of headings (e.g., #, ##, and ###) to create a clear structure.

A reproducible script should work regardless of which computer it is running on. Absolute paths (e.g., C:/Users/Username/Documents/...) are the “enemy” of reproducibility because they only exist on your specific device.
Relative paths point to a location relative to your project’s root folder.

Code# Bad: Absolute path
my_data <- read.csv("C:/Users/Username/Documents/Project/data/raw/my_data.csv")



Code# Good: Relative path using 'here'
my_data <- read.csv(here("data", "raw", "my_data.csv"))
head(my_data)

  id age condition reaction_time attention_check
1  1  61 Treatment      2.363845          passed
2  2  62 Treatment      2.255621          passed
3  3  31   Control      1.220228          passed
4  4  57 Treatment      2.870667          passed
5  5  48   Control      1.349816          passed
6  6  42 Treatment      1.701745          passed



In Python, you can achieve the same using the os module to construct relative paths:

Code# Good: Relative path using 'os' (Python`s root is the respective file directory, not the project directory)
path = os.path.join("..", "data", "raw", "my_data.csv")
my_data = pd.read_csv(path)




Clear naming is the first step toward “self-documenting code.”

Variables: Use nouns (e.g., participant_scores).
Functions: Use verbs (e.g., calculate_mean).
Style: Use snake_case (lowercase letters with underscores) for both R and Python.

Avoid generic names like data1, temp, or foo. Instead, be descriptive: raw_survey_data is much more helpful than just df.

Comments should explain why you are doing something, rather than describing the code itself. Anyone can look up what e.g. the mean() function does, but only you know why you chose to exclude certain outliers before calculating it.

Code# OVERLOAD: Describing the syntax (Avoid this)
# Defining a variable and using the subset function
cleaned_data <- subset(my_data, attention_check == "passed") # Result is saved



Code# GOOD: Explaining the context
# Removing participants who failed the attention check
cleaned_data <- subset(my_data, attention_check == "passed")



If your analysis involves random processes (like creating random groups or running a bootstrap), you must set a seed. This ensures that the “random” numbers are the same every time the script is run. this ensures reproducibility across devices and over time.

Codeset.seed(123)

sample(1:10, 5)

[1]  3 10  2  8  6




Coderandom.seed(123)

random.sample(range(1, 10), 5)

[1, 5, 9, 4, 3]




Don`t repeat yourself (DRY): If you find yourself writing the same code multiple times, it’s a sign that you should create a function. This reduces errors—if you need to change a parameter, you only have to change it in one place.

Code#' Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit
#' 
#' The function converts a temperature value from Celsius to Fahrenheit.
#' @param celsius A numeric value representing degrees Celsius.
#' @return A numeric value representing degrees Fahrenheit.
# Define the conversion function
convert_celsius_to_fahrenheit <- function(celsius) {
  # Calculate fahrenheit using the standard formula
  fahrenheit <- (celsius * 9/5) + 32
  
  # Return the final result
  return(fahrenheit)
}

# Apply the function to different values
convert_celsius_to_fahrenheit(0)   # Freezing point

[1] 32

Codeconvert_celsius_to_fahrenheit(20)  # Room temperature

[1] 68

Codeconvert_celsius_to_fahrenheit(37)  # Body temperature

[1] 98.6




Code# Define the conversion function
def convert_celsius_to_fahrenheit(celsius):
    """
The function convert_celsius_to_fahrenheit converts a temperature value from Celsius to Fahrenheit using the standard formula F = (C × 9/5) + 32.
Arguments: @param celsius: A numeric value (or vector/series) representing the temperature in degrees Celsius.
Return value: A numeric value (or vector/series) representing the corresponding temperature in degrees Fahrenheit
    """
    # Calculate fahrenheit using the standard formula
    fahrenheit = (celsius * 9/5) + 32
    
    # Return the final result
    return fahrenheit

# Apply the function to different values
print(convert_celsius_to_fahrenheit(0))   # Freezing point

32.0

Codeprint(convert_celsius_to_fahrenheit(20))  # Room temperature

68.0

Codeprint(convert_celsius_to_fahrenheit(37))  # Body temperature

98.6




Normally, your code runs sequentially (one task after another). If you have a computer with multiple CPU cores, parallelization allows you to run multiple independent tasks at the same time, significantly reducing execution time for “embarrassingly parallel” tasks.
You can use it, for example, in R (Simulations) if you need to run a lot of independent iterations of the same model or in Python if you want to make many requests to APIs or networks in general.
Consult your supervisor for more information on how to implement parallelization in your specific project, as it can be complex and may not be necessary for all analyses. # ToDo

Plots and figures of your final thesis should never be edited manually but always within the script only. This ensures they are fully reproducible. You can save your plots and figures directly from your script using the ggsave() function in R or the savefig() function in Python.

Code# Create a simple plot
plot <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()

# Show plot
plot






Code# Save the plot
ggsave(here("outputs", "figs", "mtcars_plot.png"), plot = plot, width = 6, height = 4)




Code# Create a simple plot
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [10, 20, 15], marker='o')

# Show plot
plt.show()






Code
# Save the plot (Python`s root is in the respective file directory, not the project directory)
plt.savefig(os.path.join("..", "outputs", "figs", "own_example_plot.png"))









If you want to write mathematical formulas or just simple equations in your report (e.g., to explain a statistical model), you can use LaTeX syntax directly within your Quarto document: $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$.
For standalone formulas on a new line, use double dollar signs:
$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$$
When rendered, this will display the formula in a professional mathematical format like \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon\).

In-line code allows you to include the results of code directly within your text. This is particularly useful for reporting specific values, such as means, standard deviations, or p-values, without having to create a separate code chunk for it. In R, you can use the r syntax to include in-line code.
This document was last rendered on the 2026-04-14.






Note



To better understand the source code behind the in-line code and other style related code syntax it is recommended to click on the </>Code-button in the top right corner of this page and select “Show source code”. This will show you the source code of this the respective page.



At the end of your report, always include a summary of your system and software versions. This is vital for troubleshooting if a package update or software update breaks your code in the future.

CodesessionInfo()

R version 4.5.2 (2025-10-31 ucrt)
Platform: x86_64-w64-mingw32/x64
Running under: Windows 11 x64 (build 22631)

Matrix products: default
  LAPACK version 3.12.1

locale:
[1] LC_COLLATE=German_Switzerland.utf8  LC_CTYPE=German_Switzerland.utf8   
[3] LC_MONETARY=German_Switzerland.utf8 LC_NUMERIC=C                       
[5] LC_TIME=German_Switzerland.utf8    

time zone: Europe/Zurich
tzcode source: internal

attached base packages:
[1] stats     graphics  grDevices datasets  utils     methods   base     

other attached packages:
[1] ggplot2_4.0.2 here_1.0.2    furrr_0.4.0   future_1.70.0

loaded via a namespace (and not attached):
 [1] Matrix_1.7-5       gtable_0.3.6       jsonlite_2.0.0     compiler_4.5.2    
 [5] renv_1.2.0         Rcpp_1.1.1         parallel_4.5.2     globals_0.19.1    
 [9] scales_1.4.0       png_0.1-9          yaml_2.3.12        fastmap_1.2.0

[17] knitr_1.50         rprojroot_2.1.1    RColorBrewer_1.1-3 rlang_1.2.0

[29] rappdirs_0.3.4     lifecycle_1.0.5    vctrs_0.7.3        evaluate_1.0.5    
[33] glue_1.8.0         farver_2.1.2       listenv_0.10.1     codetools_0.2-20  
[37] parallelly_1.46.1  rmarkdown_2.30     purrr_1.2.2        tools_4.5.2       
[41] htmltools_0.5.9   




Codesession_info.show()

-----
matplotlib          3.10.8
pandas              3.0.2
session_info        1.0.0
-----
Python 3.12.13 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Mar  5 2026, 16:36:12) [MSC v.1944 64 bit (AMD64)]
Windows-11-10.0.22631-SP0
-----
Session information updated at 2026-04-14 16:04









Note



The IDE RStudio offers an automatic formatter. Use Ctrl + Shift + A (Windows) or Cmd + Shift + A (Mac) to reformat a highlighted code block.

title: Syntax
text:
Plots and figures of your final thesis should never be edited manually but always within the script only. This ensures they are fully reproducible. You can save your plots and figures directly from your script using the ggsave() function in R or the savefig() function in Python.

Code# Create a simple plot
plot <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal()

# Show plot
plot






Code# Save the plot
ggsave(here("outputs", "figs", "mtcars_plot.png"), plot = plot, width = 6, height = 4)




Code# Create a simple plot
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [10, 20, 15], marker='o')

# Show plot
plt.show()






Code
# Save the plot (Python`s root is in the respective file directory, not the project directory)
plt.savefig(os.path.join("..", "outputs", "figs", "own_example_plot.png"))

title: Syntax
text:
If you want to write mathematical formulas or just simple equations in your report (e.g., to explain a statistical model), you can use LaTeX syntax directly within your Quarto document: $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$.
For standalone formulas on a new line, use double dollar signs:
$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$$
When rendered, this will display the formula in a professional mathematical format like \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon\).

title: Syntax
text:
In-line code allows you to include the results of code directly within your text. This is particularly useful for reporting specific values, such as means, standard deviations, or p-values, without having to create a separate code chunk for it. In R, you can use the r syntax to include in-line code.
This document was last rendered on the 2026-04-14.






Note



To better understand the source code behind the in-line code and other style related code syntax it is recommended to click on the </>Code-button in the top right corner of this page and select “Show source code”. This will show you the source code of this the respective page.

title: Syntax
text:
At the end of your report, always include a summary of your system and software versions. This is vital for troubleshooting if a package update or software update breaks your code in the future.

title: Analysis Template
text: Download this template from the GitHub repository or via the </>Code-button in the top right corner. This template is designed to be a starting point for your analyses, following many of the best practices for reproducibility and clarity.

title: Analysis Template
text:
3.1 Loading Data
Using relative paths ensures that the script runs on any device.

Code# Example for a real file: 
my_data <- read.csv(here("data", "raw", "my_data.csv"))




Code# In Python, we go one level up to reach the project root from 'scripts/'
path = os.path.join("..", "data", "raw", "my_data.csv")
df = pd.read_csv(path)

title: Analysis Template
text:
3.2 Processing
We follow naming conventions (snake_case) and explain why we filter data.

Code# Removing cases that don't meet the attention threshold
cleaned_data <- subset(my_data, reaction_time < ATTENTION_THRESHOLD)




Code# Removing cases that don't meet the attention threshold
df_cleaned = df[df['reaction_time'] < ATTENTION_THRESHOLD].copy()

title: Analysis Template
text:
4.1 Statistical Analysis
Here we would perform our statistical tests, ensuring to report all relevant details for reproducibility.

Codemean_reaction_time <- mean(cleaned_data$reaction_time)




Codemean_reaction_time = df_cleaned['reaction_time'].mean()



To report the result I can simply use inline code. The mean reaction time is 1.49 seconds.

title: Analysis Template
text:
4.2 Plotting
According to our workflow, we create all figures within the code and save all figures into the outputs/figs/ directory.

Code# Create the plot
p <- ggplot(cleaned_data, aes(x = condition, y = reaction_time)) +
  geom_point(color = PLOT_COLOUR) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Reaction time in dependence of condition",
       caption = "Data source: Reaction test XY")

# Display
p






Code# Save using 'here' to target the project root
ggsave(here("outputs", "figs", "analysis_plot_r.png"), plot = p, width = 6, height = 4)




Code# Create the plot
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.scatter(df['condition'], df['reaction_time'], color=PLOT_COLOUR, alpha=0.7)
plt.title('Reaction time in dependence of condition')
plt.xlabel('Condition')
plt.ylabel('Reaction Time')

# Display
plt.show()






Code
# Save using os.path to target the project root
save_path = os.path.join("..", "outputs", "figs", "analysis_plot_py.png")
plt.savefig(save_path, dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')

To ensure results remain the same across devices, we set a seed.

Codeset.seed(123)
sample_values <- sample(1:100, 3)
sample_values

[1] 31 79 51




Coderandom.seed(123)
sample_values = random.sample(range(1, 101), 3)
sample_values

title: About
text:
About this site

Code1 + 1

[1] 2

title: Welcome
text:
This is the landing page of the reproducibility guide of the Department Psychology of Digitalisation at the University of Bern. This guide aims to give students an introduction to reproducible and clean code for their thesis projects.






Important



Whatever the content of this guide, please note that any guidelines from your supervisor are always superior to the guidelines provided here. This guide is intended to be a helpful resource, but it cannot replace the specific requirements and expectations of your supervisor. Always prioritize the instructions and feedback from your supervisor when working on your thesis.


This guide is hosted on GitHub and is open for contributions. If you have suggestions for improvement, please let us know by creating an issue on the GitHub repository or by contacting us directly.
The guide is inspired by the Guide to Reproducible Code from the British Ecological Society (BES).
For your orientation: The content of this guide is mostly written such that the guidelines can be followed independently for any programming language but for the IDE of RStudio. However, the examples provided focus on the programming language of R as this is the most commonly used one for data analysis in psychology. If a particular guideline applies differently to the programming language of Python, this will be marked explicitly in the text via the following information box:

Content related in particular to Python will be placed in these information boxes.

title: FAIR
text:
To make research results more reproducible, there are the so-called FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable). Research projects, including your thesis, should follow these principles wherever possible. In particular for code and software, these principles can be applied as follows:

Findable: The whole content should be easily findable. This can be achieved through the use of unique identifiers, metadata, naming conventions, and folder structures which follow guidelines and contain descriptive information about the content.
Accessible: All the material should be accessible to everyone. This can be achieved by using open access repositories, providing clear instructions for accessing the material, and ensuring that the material is available in a format that can be easily used by others. Further, all applied software should be open source and freely available to everyone.
Interoperable: Software and code should be compatible with other software products and data to facilitate collaboration and the exchange of information. This can be achieved through the use of open standards and interfaces.
Reusable: Software should be designed so that it can be easily reused by other researchers. This includes ensuring that it is clearly documented, allowing for modifications to be built upon it, or making it integratable into other software.

The following pages provide practical guidance on implementing the FAIR principles in your work. While this is not an exhaustive list, it serves as a robust starting point for making your research more transparent and reproducible. As you write your thesis, keep the FAIR principles in mind and look for opportunities to align your workflow even more closely with them.

title: Version control
text:
Fundamentals
Version control is the systematic process of documenting every change made to your scripts, data, or outputs. It allows you to trace when, why, and by whom specific changes were made. Most importantly, it acts as a safety net, allowing you to restore previous versions of your work if something goes wrong.


GitHub
GitHub is essentially a specialized cloud provider—similar to Google Drive or OneDrive—but designed specifically for code and version control. In GitHub, projects are organized into repositories, which function as project folders containing all your files and their history.
By using the GitHub Desktop application, you can sync a folder on your local device with a repository on GitHub. Your progress is tracked through commits. Unlike a standard ‘save,’ a commit creates a permanent snapshot of your project at a specific point in time. Instead of overwriting files, Git records the precise differences between versions.
Once you have committed your changes locally, you can ‘upload’ them to the cloud using a push. Conversely, if the repository has been updated (e.g., by a collaborator or your supervisor), you can download those updates using a pull. Always pull before you start working and push once you finish a task to keep everyone in sync.


Git
While GitHub Desktop is a great starting point, the true power lies in the underlying software called Git. Git is the ‘engine’ that powers both GitHub and the Desktop app. After a one-time setup, you can connect Git directly to your programming environment (like RStudio). This allows you to perform commits, pushes, and pulls directly within the same window where you write your code, removing the need to switch back and forth between different applications. This integration streamlines your workflow and makes versioning a seamless part of your coding habit.






Note



You can find instructions for the one-time setup of Git, GitHub, R, and the connection between these here.








Note



This website itself was build with the open source programming language of R, the IDE of RStudio, Git and GitHub for version control. You can find the corresponding repository here.




.gitignore
A .gitignore file prevents Git from tracking specific files or folders, ensuring they are never uploaded to GitHub. To use it, create a text file named .gitignore in your project root and list any files or patterns (e.g., *.csv, .Rhistory) you wish to exclude. This is a critical security step for protecting sensitive participant data and maintaining a clean repository by excluding unnecessary large files which could break the upload.






Note



Note that on the GitHub repository you can find the respective .gitignore-file for this website project. You can use it as a template for your own project and modify it according to your needs.




renv
renv (Reproducible Environments) records the exact versions of all R packages you are using.This ensures that anyone who wants to reproduce your work can simply install the same package versions, preventing issues that arise from updates or changes in package behavior. By using renv, you can create a snapshot of your project’s dependencies, making it easier to share and collaborate on your code while maintaining reproducibility.
You initialize renv in your project by running renv::init() in the R console. This will create a renv.lock file that contains all the package versions you are using. Whenever you install a new package or update an existing one, make sure to run renv::snapshot() to update the lock file. When someone else wants to reproduce your work, they can run renv::restore() to install the exact package versions specified in the lock file.






Note



It is also possible to combine .gitignore and renv by adding the renv/library/ folder to the .gitignore file. This way, the actual package files are not tracked by Git, but the renv.lock file is still included in the repository, allowing others to restore the same environment without uploading large package files.
